senses.definition: tending to renounce or characterized by renunciation
examples: The renunciative stance of the group surprised many observers.
A renunciative attitude can shape one's personal philosophy.

senses.definition: a person who renounces or gives up something, especially a claim or right
examples: The renunciator waived his claims to the estate.
In religious orders, a renunciator may surrender certain privileges.

senses.definition: having or expressing renunciation; showing rejection or surrender of claims or rights
examples: A renunciatory letter signaled the end of his legal battle.
Her renunciatory remarks surprised the audience.

senses.definition: an anatomical term meaning a renunculus is not a standard English lexical item; may appear as a pseudo-Latin form or misspelling related to renunci- roots
examples: No common senses are found for renunculus in modern English; it appears infrequently outside technical or mistaken usage.

senses.definition: to reverse or invert something; to turn back or flip in position or direction
examples: The artist renverses the perspective to create a disorienting effect.
The camera renverses the scene, making the foreground recede.

senses.definition: describing something that has been renversed (reversed) or inverted
examples: The renversed colors created an otherworldly look.
A renversed perspective altered the viewer's interpretation.

senses.definition: the act of inverting or reversing; a reversal, especially in a political or social sense
examples: The renversement of power led to dramatic policy changes.
A renversement of fortunes changed the community's outlook.

senses.definition: plural of renversement; acts or instances of reversals or inversions
examples: The renversements affected several sectors of the economy.
Historical renversements shaped the era.

senses.definition: third person singular present of renverse; to invert or reverse something
examples: The market renverses expectations occasionally.
The lens renverses the image, creating a mirrored view.

senses.definition: gerund of renverse; the act of inverting or reversing
examples: Renversing the perspective changes the entire composition.
The renversing of roles surprised the participants.

senses.definition: a misspelling or rare form of renverse; no standard entry in common English dictionaries
examples: Renverst appears in some historical manuscripts but is not widely used.

senses.definition: a reference to a prior point or document; in law, a provision sending a case back to a lower court or another authority
examples: The contract includes a renvoi to the original governing law.
In legal writing, renvoi can refer a matter to a higher court.

senses.definition: plural of renvoi; references or referrals in legal or scholarly texts
examples: The renvois in the treaty clarified jurisdiction.
Scholars discuss the renvois within the footnotes.

senses.definition: an archaic or bibliographic spelling of renvoi; not standard in modern usage
examples: The manuscript uses renvoy in places where renvoi is now standard.

senses.definition: plural of renvoyer; a person sent to another place; in some contexts, a officer tasked with sending back or delegating
examples: Renvoiys conducted the delegation across borders (note: historical spelling).

senses.definition: a surname or place name; may refer to people or locations named Renwick
examples: Renwick is a surname found in several countries.
Renwick House in the town is a local landmark.

senses.definition: likely a misspelling or rare form; not a standard English word
present participle of a nonstandard form; not common in modern usage
examples: Renying appears in some dialect writings, but is not widespread.

senses.definition: short for 'recreation', 'recreation equipment operator', or 'reo' as a company acronym; without context, meaning is unclear
examples: The word reo is ambiguous without domain context.

senses.definition: to object again or to reframe an object conceptually; though not standard, could be used in specialized discourse
examples: They chose to reobject the design to emphasize accessibility.

senses.definition: past tense of reobject; to object again or to reframe an object conceptually
examples: The committee reobjected the proposal after further review.

senses.definition: present participle of reobject; the act of objecting again or reframing an object conceptually
examples: They are reobjecting the framework in the latest draft.

senses.definition: the process of making something more objective or aligning it with objectivity; a rare or technical term
examples: The theory discusses reobjectivization in its methodology.

senses.definition: to make something more objective or to frame it in objective terms
examples: scholars attempt to reobjectivize the data for clarity

senses.definition: plural of reobject; objects reframed or redefined in an objective context
examples: The exhibit featured reobjects that challenged viewers' assumptions.
